Thanks for the review @ljharb! Updated based on your feedback:

1. **Now using lodash's existing `isNative`** - when converting the library, `placeholder` is the lodash object so I use `placeholder.isNative` when available, with a simple regex fallback for single-function conversion.

2. **Test now covers more native functions** - Added `Object.keys`, `Object.assign`, `Number.isFinite`, and `Number.isNaN` in addition to `Array.isArray`.

Force-pushed the updated commit.

I don't think this needs to be done.
>
> Lodash's isArray is a direct reference to the native Array.isArray function:
> ```
> var isArray = Array.isArray;
> ```
 Just change isArray.js to:

```js
module.exports = function isArray(x) {
    return Array.isArray(x);
}
```

Now it's not a reference.

Alternatively, you can do this isNative fix, but I think that making a new function instance instead of just having a reference to a native function prevents future bugs.
Plus, if a user is using core-js, isNative could return false for the Array.isArray polyfill, polluting the polyfill anyway.
https://github.com/novavale/lodash/pull/1

Good catch on the core-js edge case — you're right that `isNative` is an unreliable gate there.

Simplified the fix based on your feedback. Pushed an updated commit that drops the `isNative` approach entirely in favor of a cleaner invariant: **never mutate a function you don't own**.

Two changes:
1. **Placeholder** — removed `func.placeholder = placeholder`. The fp module only needs `result.placeholder` (the wrapped function), not the original. No environment detection needed.
2. **Convert** — always wrap `func` in a new function before attaching `.convert`. Previously we only did this for native functions (fragile with core-js); now we do it unconditionally.

Both cases now follow the same principle, no `isNative` required. Let me know if you see any test failures or edge cases I missed.
@novavale Just look at my solution. I think it is a lot simpler than adding this unnecessary logic. https://github.com/novavale/lodash/pull/1

Thanks @tj-commits — I appreciate the simpler framing, but I think the approaches solve different things.

Your fix wraps `isArray` individually so `lodash.fp`'s conversion logic stops attaching methods to it. That works for `isArray`, but the root issue in `_baseConvert.js` affects any function that happens to be a direct reference to a native — not just this one. A future contributor adding another native reference would hit the same bug.

Also worth noting: your PR edits `dist/lodash.js` directly, which is a generated file. The right place would be `src/isArray.js`, and even then it would need a corresponding source change + rebuild to stick.

The `_baseConvert.js` fix handles the invariant at the conversion layer: *never attach `.convert` or `.placeholder` to a function you don't own*. That makes the fp module safe regardless of which lodash functions happen to be native references, polyfilled, or wrapped downstream.

Happy to discuss further — open to merging approaches if @ljharb has a preference either way.

> Also worth noting: your PR edits `dist/lodash.js` directly, which is a generated file. The right place would be `src/isArray.js`, and even then it would need a corresponding source change + rebuild to stick.

> A future contributor adding another native reference would hit the same bug.

Just make sure that a future contributor doesn't make something a native reference, or you can keep your change anyway, but having `isArray.js` not be a reference to a native makes sense anyway.

Merged — thanks for the fix. Your change is now on our `fix/fp-native-pollution` branch alongside the `_baseConvert.js` update.

The two together cover both layers:
- **`lodash.js`** (your fix): `isArray` is no longer a direct native reference, so core-js polyfills can't be polluted at the source.
- **`_baseConvert.js`** (our fix): the fp conversion layer never attaches `.convert` or `.placeholder` to functions it doesn't own, regardless of how they're defined.

Looking forward to the advisory going public — happy to reference it formally in the PR if that helps the merge case with @ljharb.

Hi @ljharb, following up on your earlier questions about this PR.

**Does lodash have existing isNative logic?**
Yes. The codebase has isNative checks and the PR uses that same pattern as a defensive guard.

**Should we test more functions than just Array.isArray?**
The updated test covers: Array.isArray, Object.keys, Object.assign, Number.isFinite, Number.isNaN.

The two-layer fix: (1) lodash.js wraps isArray so its not a direct native reference, (2) _baseConvert.js gates .convert/.placeholder attachment using isNative detection.

Is there anything else you would like to see before this can move forward?

Luckily the commands to regenerate this file are captured in the header comment.

It's hinky bc it requires using @bnjmnt4n's forked lodash-cli (as the lodas-cli is archived, but changes were needed to it, and we need those changes here as well)

1. Checkout the branch you want to compare against
2. Install the lodash-cli fork: `npm i -D github:bnjmnt4n/lodash-cli`
3. Copy main's lodash.js as the source: `git show main:dist/lodash.js > node_modules/lodash/lodash.js` (make sure you've got latest main!)
4. Run the build command from the header comment:
   - AMD: `npx lodash exports=amd -d -o ./main.js`
5. Check the diff: `git diff main.js`

Caveats which are indicative of the issues w/ treating main's lodash.js as source of truth, but it currently having drifted slightly:
- The regenerated file will have VERSION from main's source (4.17.21), so you'll need to bump it after (bumping version in main would prevent this, but we were saving it for last)
- The setCacheHas JSDoc has a bug in main (`@returns {number}` should be `@returns {boolean}`) - this regresses the fix that was in the distribution branches
- For minified files, postprocess.js in lodash-cli replaces the full license header with a short one, so those need manual handling to replace it with the longer one (remnant of the license being updated manually, but the cli not getting the update)

Doesn't seem like a great API function signature. If I look at code and see `_.toNumber(something)`, that's clear. If I see `_.toNumber(something, somethingElse)`, I'm not sure what is going on. I'm not sure about the performance implications, but it is probably better to use an options object in this case: `_.toNumber(something, {defaultValue: 0})`
@Trott thanks for the input. I have made the necessary changes

> @Trott thanks for the input. I have made the necessary changes

I'm not a lodash maintainer or anything, so bear that in mind, but I would imagine that the tests should be added to `test/number-coercion-methods.spec.js` rather than creating a separate test file just for additional `toNumber()` tests. That also raises the question of whether symmetric changes should be made to the API of `toFinite()`, `toInteger()`, and  `toSafeInteger()`. But those never return `NaN` so I'm not sure it makes sense.

That in turn raises the issue of whether this API adjustment makes sense. It seems like it would typically be used to return `0` instead of `NaN`, but that's already what `toFinite()` does, and `toFinite()` has the added bonus of returning usable values for `Infinity` and `-Infinity`.

@eragon11 Why not `isNumber`? Aren't all numbers non-empty?

> @eragon11 Why not `isNumber`? Aren't all numbers non-empty?

I'd argue quite the opposite. A number is a primitive value, not an object, so it can never "contain" anything making it always empty.

The `isEmpty` function is plainly aimed at "collection"-like objects. Anything that isn't one should be considered empty by definition.

It's reasonable that instances of `Object` only take on this "collection"-like characteristic if they have their own properties. Properties gained from the prototype chain shouldn't (and do not) qualify.

```javascript
const a = {};
"toString" in a;               // true
Object.hasOwn(a, "toString");  // false
_.isEmpty(a);                  // true

const b = {
  prop: "value",
};
"prop" in b;               // true
Object.hasOwn(b, "prop");  // true
_.isEmpty(b);              // false
```

I think it should filter using [`isNil`](https://lodash.com/docs/4.17.15#isNil).

@camsteffen the intention here is to have `null` be the one intended 'filter' value and only check for that, because other falsy values, such as `''`, `0`, etc. can be returned intentionally. In my opinion, a `null` signifies an intended non-value, which is a signal to the function to filter, while an `undefined` signifies that a value is missing, which is a valid return type. Furthermore for the Typescript typing, I would prefer to type the filtermap function as `(val: T) => U | null`, in which there is one clear return type that signals it should be filtered out, rather than the ugly `(val: T) => U | null | undefined`, which does not specify to the user if their filtering function should return `null` or `undefined`. Please let me know what you think and I'd like to get this merged.

`isNil` does not return true for all falsey values, just `null` and `undefined`. I agree with your description of `null` and `undefined`, but I still think `filterMap` should filter both out just because it would be the [least surprising](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principle_of_least_astonishment) behavior IMO.

```javascript
import upperFirst from './upperFirst.js'
import words from './words.js'
import toString from './toString.js'

/**
 * Converts `string` to [pascal case](https://wiki.c2.com/?PascalCase).
 *
 * @since 4.0.1
 * @category String
 * @param {string} [string=''] The string to convert.
 * @returns {string} Returns the pascal cased string.
 * @see lowerCase, kebabCase, snakeCase, startCase, upperCase, upperFirst
 * @example
 *
 * pascalCase('Foo Bar')
 * // => 'FooBar'
 *
 * pascalCase('--foo-bar--')
 * // => 'FooBar'
 *
 * pascalCase('__FOO_BAR__')
 * // => 'FooBar'
 */
const pascalCase = (string) => (
  words(toString(string).replace(/['\u2019]/g, '')).reduce((result, word, index) => {
    word = word.toLowerCase()
    return result + upperFirst(word);
  }, '')
)

export default pascalCase
```

I've seen this simplification in other code bases too. I'll think on it.

This simplification looks correct to me. (I was just looking at this code and thinking the same thing, and came here to see if there was already a PR for it.) It'd be good to have it merged, because it makes this function both more efficient and much simpler to understand.

Here's one way of thinking about why the existing code has the same behavior as the new code in this PR. The existing code says this:
```js
  if (Object.getPrototypeOf(value) === null) {
    return true
  }
  let proto = value		
  while (Object.getPrototypeOf(proto) !== null) {		
    proto = Object.getPrototypeOf(proto)		
  }		
  return Object.getPrototypeOf(value) === proto
```
The point is that the expression in that `return` at the end is asking precisely the question: did the loop body run exactly one time? And so the two `return` statements together are asking: would that loop run for either zero or one times?

Which is exactly what the new code says:
```js
  const proto = Object.getPrototypeOf(value)
  return proto === null || Object.getPrototypeOf(proto) === null
```

To see that the `return` at the end of the existing code is asking precisely whether the loop body ran exactly once, the main point is:
* On the one hand, after the loop body runs the first time, that condition `Object.getPrototypeOf(value) === proto` is true.
  * (It always runs at least once, because the first `return` means we don't get this far if it would run zero times.)
* If the condition `Object.getPrototypeOf(value) === proto` is ever true again, then that later value of `proto` is `===`-equal to the one we had after the first iteration.
  * That'd be a cycle in the prototype chain. I think that's forbidden by the spec.
  * But even if it can happen, it'd mean this is an infinite loop, and we'd never reach the final `return`.
* So if we get to the final `return` and the condition is true, we must have run the loop body exactly once.

---

That reasoning may look a little complicated. But all the complication is really from the existing code: the new code is much simpler and easier to understand. Merging this would mean that nobody else has to work through that reasoning again. :slightly_smiling_face:

I want to  be careful endorsing this as any kind of real issue. I accepted the previous PR as kind of a throw away thought. However, I believe it's mostly academic and it's something we've not supported in the past. Traditionally, if you mutate `Object.prototype` you've YOLO'd out of our support. That said if you fix this, please fix it for every-single-place we accept options in the code base then I can see this as having at least the value of providing consistent expectations throughout our methods.

@jdalton I would agree that an application intentionally carelessly mutating Object.prototype should expect that things break.

For this particular issue, my concern is when an application _unintentionally_ has a prototype pollution vulnerability, such as any application that used a slightly older version of lodash and used e.g. deep merge or clone would be. It's a hard problem to avoid in large code bases, as `anyObject[a][b] = c` is a prototype pollution for user controlled a, b and c, though it rarely presents itself as that "obvious". Most implementations of deep merge or deep-set-by-path that I've seen have been vulnerable to this at some point, which includes lodash.

`Function/eval` are super high value targets, and an application that has a prototype pollution bug and subsequently invokes lodash.template now has a remote code execution bug. The pollution is a vulnerability by itself that warrants fixing in the application, but at least we can make it harder for an attacker to escalate to arbitrary code execution.

Giving the same treatment to all other options might be a larger breaking change and is more likely to hit legitimate uses of options that inherit something.

At the very least I'd urge accepting the change to the sanitizing of sourceURL. If variable and imports are polluted, they still seem to be masked by the default `settings`, whereas sourceURL is not. sourceURL is also more likely to be coming from something user provided even without a pollution issue.

I'm happy to make a PR that only does the sourceURL sanitizing.
I created https://github.com/lodash/lodash/pull/4518 which I hope is easier to accept. :)

> The pollution is a vulnerability by itself that warrants fixing in the application, but at least we can make it harder for an attacker to escalate to arbitrary code execution.

Existing JS APIs are susceptible to this today including descriptors for `Object.defineProperty`/`Reflect.defineProperty`, trap handlers for `new Proxy(o, ...)`, and array index property access.

> I created #4518 which I hope is easier to accept. :)

For #4518 you can continue to preserve the `hasOwnProperty` checks.

> Existing JS APIs are susceptible to this today including descriptors for Object.defineProperty/Reflect.defineProperty, trap handlers for new Proxy(o, ...), and array index property access.

I'm not sure I'm following. With defineProperty and proxy traps you can certainly change objects, but I'm not aware of it accepting strings in an options object that in turn execute as Javascript,  which could be susceptible to a default being read off the prototype?

An attacker that is able to influence objects through these calls would already be in a pretty privileged position, much more so than someone just able to pass a JSON object that passes through pollution-prone merge-like calls.

> I'm not sure I'm following. 

If you do `Object.prototype.enumerable = true` then if you don't provide the descriptor property `enumerable` to the `Object.defineProperty` method it will search the prototype of the descriptor object you provide and use the value found instead. Many folks don't provide the full descriptor or handle traps since they usually default to `undefined`.
@jdalton Ok, so we close this in favour of #4518 ? :)

As a follow up. I'm looking to replace all `whateverFunc.call` and `whateverFunc.apply` calls with `Reflect.apply` calls. So for example:

```js
whateverFunc.call(this, foo)
```
would be turned into
```js
Reflect.apply(whateverFunc, this, [foo])
```
and
```js
whateverFunc.apply(this, args)
```
would be turned into
```js
Reflect.apply(whateverFunc, this, args)
```

> As a follow up. I'm looking to replace all whateverFunc.call and whateverFunc.apply calls with Reflect.apply calls

I see some (small) drawbacks of such changes:
* Worse minification
* Instantiation of an extra array when replacing call methods

I'm fine with the extra array. The win here is consistent use of the `Reflect` API (basically if it can be done with it we'll be doing it). Translates well to eventual `Proxy` use too. Minification with Gzip will gobble that up (so not as big a deal). Gzip loves repeated code bits.

Hi, @jdalton! 
In current implementation `debounce` remembers only arguments list from the most recent call. But in some situation arguments list from the earliest call have to be applied instead. For example:
Standard behavior:
```
500 Put to stack callback(1)
1002 Put to stack callback(2)
1502 Put to stack callback(3)
2005 Put to stack callback(4)
2501 Run callback(4)
2506 Put to stack callback(5)
3012 Put to stack callback(6)
3513 Put to stack callback(7)
4014 Put to stack callback(8)
4509 Run callback(8)
4515 Put to stack callback(9)
5016 Put to stack callback(10)
5517 Put to stack callback(11)
6519 Run callback(11)
```
Option `{lazy: true}` effects behavior this way:
```
500 Put to stack callback(1)
1002 Put to stack callback(2)
1502 Put to stack callback(3)
2005 Put to stack callback(4)
2501 Run callback(1)
2506 Put to stack callback(5)
3012 Put to stack callback(6)
3513 Put to stack callback(7)
4014 Put to stack callback(8)
4509 Run callback(5)
4515 Put to stack callback(9)
5016 Put to stack callback(10)
5517 Put to stack callback(11)
6519 Run callback(9)
```

To play devil's advocate, should we expose an option to opt-out of RAF, to ~get~ guarantee the existing behavior?

Another consideration regarding using RAF by default:

> The number of callbacks is usually 60 times per second, but will generally match the display refresh rate in most web browsers as per W3C recommendation. **`requestAnimationFrame()` calls are paused in most browsers when running in background tabs or hidden `<iframe>`s in order to improve performance and battery life.**

Thanks @stefanmaric!

I'm OK with our version of `xor`. I think it makes more sense for common usage and can't see the example case, `_.xor( a, b, c )`, with `5` in the result as being what the user intended.

@jdalton I understand the point; finding the mutually exclusive elements of a collection of sets seems a more recurrent scenario than finding their actual symmetric difference.

But in the beginning `xor` was _mathematically correct_ and only after multiple years one person complained about it – it took way less time for someone (myself) to complain about the opposite.

Wouldn't it better to use the right semantics and provide the user with the tools for them to achieve what they want? It may be a new utility or we might educate them as well, via the docs. What `xor` currently does can be achieved with:

```javascript
const exclusion = (...arrays) =>
  _.flatMap(arrays, x => _.difference(x, ..._.without(arrays, x)))
```

Just saying that it may be worth it to stand on the shoulders of mathematical foundations and that it shouldn't be hard to workaround users' expectations.

Just to be clear, technically `sortedUniq` work if the array is so that it guarantees that all items with the same value are consecutive, even if the array is not sorted. Is that correct?

In the case of `sortedUniqBy`, it works if the array is so that all items for which the iteratee function yields a certain value, appear consecutively in the array.

I don't mean to imply with this that the documentation should go to this level of details. It's just that I'm using `sortedUniqBy` in a situation where I've got an array of objects, all of which have `name` and `id`: I want to sort by name, and then remove duplicates by id.

```javascript
// using the fp versions cause the argument order is preferable
sortedUniqBy('id', sortBy('name', array))
```

After the array is sorted by name, all items with the same id are guaranteed to appear consecutively, but not necessarily in order by `id`.

My guess is you object to the word 'invalid'.  How about being really explicit in the description?

``` Gets the value at `path` of `object`, or get `undefined` if the path cannot be evaluated.  If the resolved value is `undefined`, the `defaultValue` is returned in its place.```

This gets rid of comments on the default parameter, avoids 'invalid' and is explicit.  Added the commits.

Hi @jfmengels!

markdown-doctest only checks that the examples can be executed without throwing an error. It does nothing to actually test the correctness of the code. I had originally intended when I set out to write markdown-doctest to check that the output matched, but so far the simplicity of not doing that has been a boon.

It's intended to mimic the experience a user will have when copying and running code examples. You'd be surprised by how many projects have documentation that is littered with syntax errors!

I am assuming that a cloned DataView should re-create the underlying view-able buffer, and then return a new DataView w/ the byteOffset now 'reset' to 0 with the new buffer as it's contents.

Am I making too many assumptions?

My thoughts on how this should behave are:
- Considering `DataView` doesn't contain much more than a couple integers (roughly speaking):
  1. **Pointer to memory addr** - potentially referencing a huge ArrayBuffer
  2. **Integer byteOffset** - which might limit what the DataView sees to, let's say only 8 bytes, out of perhaps many MBs.
  3. **Integer Length** - which reminds me, I forgot to account for this :astonished: 
- Would it be better to rely on `clone vs. cloneDeep` to achieve something like:
  - `_.cloneDeep(dv)` would copy the underlying buffer, resetting the offset & length? 
  - `_.clone(dv)` would simply `return new DataView(dv.buffer, dv.byteOffset, dv.byteLength)`. I think this seems a little pointless though - and it may not be what a typical user would expect?

The idea is if it's a shallow clone then just the view is cloned leaving the original array buffer in place. If it's a deep clone then the view and the buffer are cloned. undefined
```
nesh> buf = new Buffer('');
<Buffer >
nesh> buf.length
0
nesh> u.isArrayLike(buf);
true
nesh> u.isEmpty(buf)
false
```

I like the `array.pop()` as it was. That example hits a use case devs ask about. The issue here is both allow mutating the value but really `tap` is for mutation and `thru` is for replacing. So the wording should reflect that `tap` is for mutation and `thru` is for replacing.

I don't think mutation of this sort is a good model to espouse in general. A chaining pipeline is easier to reason about if each step in the pipeline is a function that takes the current value and returns a new value to the next step.

`_.tap` for mutation and `_.thru` for replacement forces a conceptual divide between altering objects and primitive data types. There's no way to "mutate" a string using `_.tap`, you have to use `_.thru`.

It seems conceptually cleaner to recommend `_.thru` for any alteration of the intermediate value, and `_.tap` for side effects.

Another thing that bothers me about the `array.pop()` example is that there are better ways to do that (calling `.initial` directly on the chain). I know that it's a contrived example of how `_.tap` can be used, but still...

I think my intent behind this PR might be too opinionated toward FP ideas of pure functions and immutability (where practical in JS), which might not be suitable for recommendation in the docs.

I'll do a compromise while still maintaining your commit cred.

@gibson042 Updated in   fad29c4 with corrected float scaling, `+string` style instead of `parseInt`/`parseFloat` to match, and new test cases that failed before.

One more note: there's still a tricky case that, intuitively, this method still seems to hiccup on (note: so does lodash's current method and a few others I've tried) – but I think might actually be the technically-correct-in-floating-point-land result:

```
> _.range(0, 7/3, 1/3);
[ 0,
  0.3333333333333333,
  0.6666666666666666,
  1,
  1.3333333333333333,
  1.6666666666666665,
  2,
  2.333333333333333 ]
```

I'm not concerned with intermediate inexact terms – like `5/3` != `5 * (1/3)` – that seems unavoidable whether we sum or multiply, even with error correction. But rather what that leads to: we've picked up an extra result – intuitively there should be 7 items, not 8. It's because of `n`, which after calculating `(7/3) / (1/3)` to be `7.000000000000001` and running `ceil` on that, tells us we should produce 8 results.

Even if we compared the last item to `end`, it'd still tell us everything is OK, because:

```
> 7 * (1/3) < 7/3
true
```

So technically, that last item "fits" within the requested range, and the user should have just specified `7 * (1/3)` as their `end`.

One option that fixes this specific case, but introduces a subtraction (and thus potential error): calculate a sentinel, `end - step`, and on the last iteration, if a previous result has already met or exceeded the sentinel, don't append the last result. Equivalent to: use `floor` instead of `ceil` and after the loop, check if we have room for one more `step`.

I'm going to punt on this for now as it creeps into other methods and complicates things for what's really an edge case concern. I'm ok tackling it in the rounding methods because we have a param explicitly for `precision` but elsehwere I'm for keeping it simple.

Ok, that's part of a discussion I had today with a colleague. `object.hasOwnProperty('a.b')` is:
- slow, as it goes through the proto chain (better use `Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(object, 'a.b')` which is not so nice)
- unsafe, as it can be overridden (as mentioned [in the underscore doc](http://underscorejs.org/#has))

I think the lodash `_.has` function is just not the same use as the orginal one.

``` js
_.has({ 'a': { 'b': true } }, ['a.b'])
```

``` js
js > var demethodize = require('demethodize')

true

false
```

We avoid a `setTimeout` of `0` because I believe old Chrome could end up blocking the UI thread with them. I avoid `setImmediate` because there's no clean way to cancel them with `clearTimeout`. 

There is `runInContext` which allows you to customize.

```
var defer = require('lodash').runInContext({ 'setTimeout': setImmediate }).defer;
```


despite `3 < null // false`

@jdalton Pascal case is not just camel case + upper first, because in case the first word is a 2 letter acronym, both letters will be capitalized, for example `I/O Status` will be `ioStatus` in camel case and `IOStatus` in Pascal case

Please implement this :)

@khawajaumarfarooq -- how does that rule work? Presumably `to me` -> `ToMe`. Does it depend on the capitalization of the original? So... `XXX YYY` -> `XXxYyy` ? (Why just 2 letter acronyms?) Note that in camel case, `i/o status` -> `iOStatus` so the example you gave works with camel case + upper first.

@shaunc This might be C#/.NET specific, but the rule there is that acronyms that are 3 or more characters go from, for example, `xml` to `Xml`, whereas acronyms like `io` go to `IO` when going from camel case to Pascal case.

Some examples:

- `ioPort` becomes `IOPort`
- `xmlDocument` becomes `XmlDocument`
- `htmlClient becomes `HtmlClient`

An exception is sometimes made for `id`, which is Pascal-ed to `Id`, although more recently, I have been tending to Pascal it to `ID`, since I like to write something like `userIDs` instead of `userIds` for variables holding enumerable types.
`i/o status` should be `ioStatus` in camel case and `IOStatus` in Pascal case.
@khawajaumarfarooq You can't really say what is an acronym without making a list it. The real possible implementation is in @jdalton comment.

@khawajaumarfarooq That's how you use it, what about how I use it? What about how other peoples uses it? For me, the solution that @jdalton provides is not perfect, but is the best, and I think that even if _.pascalCase gets implemented, it would most probably be like this. `_` has no way to know what a person, or a group call an acronym, so there is no way to follow your rule, unless is like @falsyvalues said, and make yourself a list. And you can do it, and should do. Cause it would be perfect for your usage, and if you need another acronym, then you just has to add it to the list.

There's no doubt that proper implementation of Pascal case will need a list of 2 letter acronyms (because 3+ letter acronyms get treated as a word). There aren't that many 2-letter words in the English language, so a list to figure out if a 2-letter combo is a word, and possibly a way to over-ride the list to cater to customizations. This is probably relevant to camel-case as well because I have never seen `iOStatus` as valid camel-case.

Anyway, if the majority here doesn't feel the need, that's fine.

```ts
import * as _ from 'lodash'

interface LoDashMixins extends _.LoDashStatic {
  pascalCase(string?: string): string
}

_.mixin({ pascalCase: _.flow(_.camelCase, _.upperFirst) })

const mixedLodash = <LoDashMixins>_
export { mixedLodash as _ }
```

Hi @melck! Thanks for the pull request however I don't like using the `-` or `+` prefix as that limits key names.

I choose a prefix system but it can be done otherwise. It's above all the ability to quickly sort and avoids reversing the array that's interesting here.

I can change it if you know what you want.

It's a fast way to determine if we need to inverse the index of same values for have the same results as "sortBy / reverse". I'm not good in english so i will show you by exemple

``` javascript
var datas = [
  {a: 1, b:1, c:1, d:1},
  {a: 2, b:1, c:1, d:2},
  {a: 3, b:1, c:1, d:3},
  {a: 2, b:2, c:2, d:4},
  {a: 1, b:1, c:1, d:5},
  {a: 3, b:2, c:2, d:6},
  {a: 3, b:3, c:2, d:6},
];



/*  
    Possible cases | sort by using sortBy and reverse                                              
    +a+b+c         _(datas).sortBy('c').sortBy('b').sortBy('a').value()
    +a+b-c         _(datas).sortBy('c').reverse().sortBy('b').sortBy('a').value()
    +a-b+c         _(datas).sortBy('c').reverse().sortBy('b').reverse().sortBy('a').value()
    +a-b-c         _(datas).sortBy('c').sortBy('b').reverse().sortBy('a').value()
    -a+b+c         _(datas).sortBy('c').sortBy('b').reverse().sortBy('a').reverse().value()
    -a+b-c         _(datas).sortBy('c').sortBy('b').reverse().sortBy('a').reverse().value()
    -a-b+c         _(datas).sortBy('c').reverse().sortBy('b').sortBy('a').reverse().value()
    -a-b-c         _(datas).sortBy('c').sortBy('b').sortBy('a').reverse().value()
*/

/*  
    Possible cases | Hexa  | decimal | times of reverse | reverse same values
    +a+b+c           000     0         0                  false                             
    +a+b-c           001     1         1                  true                   
    +a-b+c           010     2         2                  false                   
    +a-b-c           011     3         1                  true                   
    -a+b+c           100     4         2                  false                   
    -a+b-c           101     5         3                  true                   
    -a-b+c           110     6         2                  false                   
    -a-b-c           111     7         1                  true               


    Every times as the decimal result is odd, we need to reverse the identical results    
*/
```

At the beginning, i used logic like the following, but i have discovered a repeated pattern who permitted a more quick sort:

``` javascript
var index = -1,
  length = props ? props.length : 0,
  props = Array(typeof length == 'number' ? length : 0),
  reverse = Array(typeof length == 'number' ? length : 0),
  reverseCount = 0;

while (++index < length) {
  var
    isLastReversed = reverse[index - 1],
    isFirst = index === 0,
    regex = /^(\+|-|)(.*)?$/gi.exec(callback[index]),
    isReversed = regex[1] === "-";

  if ((isReversed && isFirst) || (_.isBoolean(isLastReversed) && (isLastReversed != isReversed))) {
    reverseCount++;
  }

  props[index] = regex[2];
  reverse[index] = isReversed;
}

reverseCount %= 2;
```

Exemple for 4 properties to sort (work for x properties):
- First column present cases.
- Second column: binary represention of each property, '1' is for property sort by desc and '0' sort by asc
- Third column: binary represention of each property with '1' when we need to reverse after a classic function sortBy
- Fourth column is decimal representation of second column.
- Fifth column represent how many times we reversed with classic way of sortBy / reverse
- Sixth column represent when same values keys need to be reversed.

| Cases | Case binary | Reverse binary | Case decimal | Times reversed | Revert |
| :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | --: |
| `+a+b+c+d` | `0000` | `0000` | `00` | `00` | NO |
| `+a+b+c-d` | `0001` | `0001` | `01` | `01` | YES |
| `+a+b-c+d` | `0010` | `0011` | `02` | `02` | NO |
| `+a+b-c-d` | `0011` | `0010` | `03` | `01` | YES |
| `+a-b+c+d` | `0100` | `0110` | `04` | `02` | NO |
| `+a-b+c-d` | `0101` | `0111` | `05` | `03` | YES |
| `+a-b-c+d` | `0110` | `0101` | `06` | `02` | NO |
| `+a-b-c-d` | `0111` | `0100` | `07` | `01` | YES |
| `-a+b+c+d` | `1000` | `1100` | `08` | `02` | NO |
| `-a+b+c-d` | `1001` | `1101` | `09` | `03` | YES |
| `-a+b-c+d` | `1010` | `1111` | `10` | `04` | NO |
| `-a+b-c-d` | `1011` | `1110` | `11` | `03` | YES |
| `-a-b+c+d` | `1100` | `1010` | `12` | `02` | NO |
| `-a-b+c-d` | `1101` | `1011` | `13` | `03` | YES |
| `-a-b-c+d` | `1110` | `1001` | `14` | `02` | NO |

I'd really like to see this feature in lodash whatever the system to sort.

Thanks for a fast response guys, couple things...

If a negated `_.isEmpty` is bad, how do you think it should be written? (I would love some expert opinion)

It's mostly just syntactic sugar, but sometimes it's nice when dealing with languages that have a less traditional syntax than JavaScript.

I'll give an example...

``` javascript
var im_empty = '';
var im_not = 'text';

if (_.isEmpty(im_empty)) {
  console.log('its true!');
}

if (!_.isEmpty(im_not)) {
  console.log('its true!');
}
```

The above feels very natural in JavaScript. And if this was only used in JavaScript it would be perfectly suitable. But why stop there...

``` coffeescript
im_empty = ''
im_not = 'text'

if _.isEmpty(im_empty)
  console.log 'its true!' 

unless _.isEmpty(im_not)
  console.log 'its true!'

```

Is this a big deal? No. But I think the following reads better.

``` coffeescript
im_empty = ''
im_not = 'text'

if _.isEmpty(im_empty)
  console.log 'its true!' 

if _.isPresent(im_not)
  console.log 'its true!'

```

Its reads nice when your if statements are checking for `true`

Ruby has many functions like this that allow you to avoid reading what almost feels like double negatives in your code. I do mostly JavaScript development now, but I remember and appreciate that feature of the libraries that the Rails community provided.

I respect you not wanting to put it in core. It's your library, your choice. Besides, it's not like I cant still use it.

@sankargorthi it captures the response/reference when getting `then` from `sometimesThenable` to use in the two statements (ex 2), rather than getting a reference to it twice (ex 1), where it may have one context in the `if` condition, and a different one when actually calling it. Hence his use of the randomized return of the `then` as a promise function.

``` javascript
if (_isThenable(sometimesThenable)) { // it may be here
  sometimesThenable.then() // but it may not be here
}
```

Whereas with

``` javascript
var then = sometimesThen.then; // we got our function
if (typeof then === "function") { // we're dealing with the same reference, so if it may be here
  then.call(sometimesThenable, ...); // then it will also be here
}
```

And now you can safely call `then` in the context of `sometimesThenable` without worrying if it is still a function.

Not really a bug. By removing an element you are shifting the entire array to the left, causing only every other element to be removed.

```
a b c d e f
^ remove 0th element

b c d e f
  ^ remove 1st element

b d e f
    ^ remove 2nd element

b d f
      ^ no 3rd element, iteration is done
```
Also many methods will grab the length of the collection before iterating, by design, so won't adjust with changes to the collection itself.

i.e.

```
_.getNestedValue(hero, 'hero.costume.mask', { msg: 'No heroes here' })
```

This is analogous to dictionary objects in Python. When you request a value with `get`, it will return `None` by default if the key doesn't exist, or you can pass an optional parameter:

```
>>> a = {"foo": "bar"}

'bar'
>>> a.get("abc")
```

btw what's your use case for this (prolly should have asked that sooner, I've seen this request come up before in Underscore's issues though).

Database relations. I'm handling appointments for a tattoo parlor. I get the `appointment` object from the REST service like this:

``` javascript
{ id:1, ... artist:2, deposit:15, payment:35 }
```

The numbers are ids in other database tables. To know what table to fetch the dependent objects from I consult a relationship table (more or less) like this:

``` javascript
{ ... artist: 'artists', deposit: 'payments', payment: 'payments' }
```

The keys are the property names and the values are the names of the tables. I want to make as few requests to the REST service as possible, ie

```
GET /payments?q=id+in+(15,35)
```

instead of 

```
GET /payments/15
GET /payments/35
```

To do this I need to be able to ask "what are all the fields that reference the `payments` table?" Or really I want to loop through all the relevant tables and ask that question about each of them. In other words, I want this object

``` javascript
{ ... artists: [ 'artist' ], payments: [ 'deposit', 'payment' ] }
```

I'd actually prefer the always-array solution -- I've found that I'm always post-processing the invert to make sure everything is an array so I can safely and easily apply further transforms to it. I'd argue that consistency is more valuable than shallowness given that this is basically a multimap (and if I'm passing in the optional parameter, you can bet that I'm looking to get a multimap back out).

Take, for instance, [Ruby's multimap.to_hash](http://www.ruby-doc.org/gems/docs/m/multimap-1.1.1/Multimap.html#method-i-to_hash) or [Guava's MultiMap.asMap](http://guava-libraries.googlecode.com/svn-history/r14/trunk/javadoc/com/google/common/collect/Multimap.html#asMap%28%29), which are basically what we're doing in js: the basic hash return is of the form key -> [values], even if there's only one value.

``` js
var reNode = RegExp('(?:^|' + path.sepEscaped + ')node(?:\\.[eE][xX][eE])?$');
```

oh, i think two ways are able to meet our needs, and two ways are not perfect.

when we use 

```
var reNode = RegExp('(?:^|' + path.sepEscaped + ')node(?:\\.[eE][xX][eE])?$');
```

some building should be successful may be failed. (in the cases, we use 'Node, nOde, ...')

when we use 

```
var reNode = RegExp('(?:^|' + path.sepEscaped + ')node(?:\\.exe)?$', 'i');
```

some building may be failed could be successful. for example, when we use 'Node build.js ...', if the `Node` is actually our node, then, the building will be successful.

for a **build tool**, i think we should not restrict arguments too tightly.

_things should be successful must be successful._

@artch you are confusing _global (symbol) namespace_ and _global state_. DI helps you to remove global state, but doesn't aim to solve global symbol namespace clashes and other namespace issues. This is what module systems like AMD (requirejs) and ES Modules do. @Nami-Doc exactly. It is an entry point of some sort.

@artch there are three packaging/module layers that people often confuse:

1/ package repository system- e.g. bower - repo of modules and their dependencies that developer uses to pull a reusable code into their application
2/ code loading system - e.g. AMD, RequireJS, CommonJS, ES Modules - how to pull files into the browser in the right order and help to prevent global namespace collisions
3/ code instantiation / component wiring / DI system - angular's DI, my experimental di.js - how to take the code that has been identified via #1 and loaded via #2 and wire it all together by instantiating components in a pluggable way

Angular modules allow you to do #3. They don't allow you to do #2 because you have to load code yourself, but they help by making code load order non-problem in some but not all cases. They definitely don't allow you to do #1.

I remember I made a conscious effort to avoid `instanceof` (though I'm a little fuzzy on the details).

The `_.isEqual` method in Underscore/Lo-Dash does a duck type for `_chain` (it should probably be `_wrapped` because wrappers can have `_chain` as `false`) and because Underscore/Lo-Dash can have multiple versions on a page (and it should work with any of them and not just the specific `_` instance that spawned it).
I think it's reasonable for devs using `_(...)` to avoid the pseudo private props used by that sugar. I'll dig around for a better inference though.
@braddunbar Also, thanks for reporting the issue/pull-request it means a lot coming from the Underscore camp.

Sure thing, glad to help out.  :)

I understand your point about multiple versions of `_` on a page but I think your premise is flawed.
1. In the majority of code I've written and read, there's only one version of `_` on the page.
2. When there are multiple versions, the code using each version usually does not communicate directly.  For instance, including a disqus widget for comments.
3. In the few instances in which they do communicate, they certainly don't pass instances of `_(…)` back and forth.

On the other hand, I very often deal with objects whose properties I don't control.  In fact, I'd say it's likely very common to wrap up a response from a jsonp endpoint which could start including a `_wrapped` property at any time.

Also, I didn't know about the `_chain` reference in `_.isEqual`, I'll check it out.  Thanks for your response/consideration!

I've simplified the inference in `_.isEqual` to use the same `_wrapped` check to at least make it consistent for now [300c9](https://github.com/bestiejs/lodash/commit/300c9aa316a31623ec681c71e798006ecd8b10e0)

I'm not too worried about false positives as `_.isEqual` used `_chain` without any reported problems. However, one solution is to double underscore it like `__proto__`, so `__wrapped__` and `__chain__`. That tends to avoid conflicts.

Ok so I've [added the double underscores](https://github.com/bestiejs/lodash/commit/945a4c81611eaf0ec30ded254b40bced583dbd36). I think that should do for now. I originally avoided modding the `_chain` and `_wrapped` out of compat concerns but I think it's edge enough to add to the [drop-in disclaimer](https://github.com/bestiejs/lodash/wiki/Drop-in-Disclaimer) once `v0.7.0` is released.

It's a feature and more consistent as other "Array" category methods like Underscore's `_first`, `_.last`, `_.initial`, and `_.zipObject` don't have fail-safes for not passing a required argument. The fail-safes are implemented in the "Collections" category methods though I'm not sure if they are really needed either. Underscore is tied to its `_.each` (a "Collections" method) when it needs to iterate and so has the side effect of its behavior bleeding into other non collections methods.
Did you run into an issue in your code where you expected an empty array?

I understand that this is not best idea to use _.flatten in this way, but anyway. In this commits I missed that underscore also flattens objects: 

``` javascript
_.flatten({'a': 1}); //[1]
```

I can do it in this PR if you want.

underscore flatten(`for in` loop):

``` javascript
_.flatten({'a': [1, 2, [3]], 'b': [1,[4],10]}); // [1,2,3,1,4,10] 
```

lodash flatten (`for` loop):

``` javascript
_.flatten({'a': [1, 2, [3]], 'b': [1,[4],10]}); // [] 
```

> In this commits I missed that underscore also flattens objects:
> 
> `_.flatten({'a': 1}); //[1]`
> I can do it in this PR if you want.

Naw, no need. The flattening of objects is undocumented/untested and an unintended side effect of Underscore's `_.each` overuse/dependency.

> yes, after drop-in replacement I found that this code fails because match returns array or null.
> 
> ``` js
> var result = _.flatten(string.match(/regexp/))[0] //result or undefined, but not throw Error;
> ```

The `string.match(/regexp/)` case is a great example of when a fail safe would be nice, because it's annoying to always do `(string.match(/regexp/) || 0)`. However, I think you juuuust happened to not run into issues as I pointed out other "Arrays" methods would have thrown errors.

I will close this for now, but will consider adding a fail safe to all "Arrays" methods.

I'd like to rename the current `isObject` to `isPrimitive`...`isObject` is a wonderfully ambiguous name, especially in a language where "everything (except primitives) is an object." I suggested this in documentcloud/underscore#421, but it would have broken backward-compatibility. I don't see the value of having an `isObject` function that checks the `[[Class]]` name, though. All user-created objects would pass this test, as well as IE's host objects. I also agree about removing `isNull` and `isUndefined`.
